segments.s551-g48: گشاید زبان literally 'opens (his) tongue' – a common poetic way to say 'speaks (against)'. زبان here means 'speech/utterance.' مرد دینش is an object-label phrase 'a man of religion' with the possessive ش 'his' attached to دین (i.e. 'his religiousness' or 'his being a man of religion'). مدار is an archaic/poetic imperative meaning 'do not reckon/regard' (here: 'do not consider him a man of religion'); this is an older usage not covered by the ordinary noun senses of مدار.
segments.s551-g25: بدان‌ای is the imperative 'بدان' (know) with the vocative clitic -ای: 'Know, O ...'. پسر is the vocative addressee 'son'. کاین is the archaic/poetic relative/demonstrative 'that/this' introducing the following noun phrase. سرای here is the noun 'saray/house, court' (poetic spelling/word-order) placed before its qualifier فریب 'deceit'.
segments.s551-g26: ندارد is the present (negative) form of 'داشتن' meaning 'it does not have' or 'there is not'. ترا is the clitic object 'to/for you' (تو + را) — note the enclitic ordering. شادمان is an adjective 'joyful/happy' and بی‌نهیب is a compound adjective 'without rebuke/without reproach'; together they describe the state being asserted (i.e. 'no deceit, [so] you may be glad without rebuke').
segments.s551-g27: نگهدار تن باش is an exhortation: باش is the imperative/sentential copula 'be' and نگهدار functions as a predicate meaning 'guardian/keeper' (understood imperatively as 'be the keeper'). تن 'body' is the direct object of that predicate. و آن خرد links 'and that wisdom' as a second thing to keep/attend to (آن = 'that/the', خرد = 'wisdom').
segments.s551-g28: چو here introduces a conditional ('if' / 'when' in classical usage). خواهی is the 2nd-person present of 'خواستن' meaning 'you wish/want'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. روزت is 'your day' (روز + possessive clitic -ت). به بد نگذرد: نگذرد is present-subjunctive-like 'not pass/go', به + بد adverbially gives the sense 'go badly' — together 'that your day not go badly'.
segments.s551-g29: چو 'when' introduces a temporal/conditional clause. بر + دین + کند: بر is the preposition 'on/upon' combining with دین 'religion' and کند 'does/makes' -> 'acts upon/upholds religion' (i.e. supports religion). شهریار is the subject 'the king'. آفرین here is a noun/nominal 'praise' or 'acclamation' used in the clause to signal approval of that act (poetic compression: 'the king [gives] praise to religion' / 'the king upholds religion, praise').
segments.s551-g30: برادر شود is 'become brothers' (شود = 'become'). The two nouns شهریاری و دین are the things becoming 'brothers' — i.e. closely allied or mutually supportive. Classical Persian often uses شود with two following nouns to state that those two things come to be united/paired.
segments.s551-g31: This first half of the correlative negation begins with نه ('not'). بی‌تخت 'without a throne' modifies the predicate that follows. شاهیست here is a nominal/predicative form referring to 'kingship' or 'sovereignty' (used in the clause about what supports religion). دینی is adjectival/nominal 'religious / of religion' (i.e. 'religion' in the abstract). به پای ('به' + پای) functions adverbially: به پای = 'at the foot/base' → 'stand firm' or 'have a firm base'; the whole clause says that religion does not stand firm without a throne.
segments.s551-g32: The second correlative negation mirrors the first: نه 'nor' + بی‌دین 'without religion' + بود (past/formal 'is/was') + شهریاری 'kingship/sovereignty' + به جای 'in place / in its place'. Grammatically, classical Persian often uses بود here for general axiomatic statements ('is/stands'); the clause means 'nor can kingship be in its place without religion.'
segments.s551-g225: بیا is a hortative/imperative 'come/let us'. تا here introduces purpose ('so that/in order that') and connects to the verb بریم. همه 'everyone/we all' modifies the subject. دست نیکی is a figurative nominal phrase 'the hand of goodness' = 'good deed(s)'; بریم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/jussive of بردن used as a hortative 'let us take/do'.
segments.s551-g226: The doubled جهان جهان is emphatic ('the whole world'); را is the definite object marker attaching to the second جهان. به بد نسپریم is a negative hortative/subjunctive: ن- + سپریم (from سپردن) = 'let us not entrust/consign to evil', with به marking the target ('to/into').
segments.s551-g227: بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative ('let us strive/try'). بر + نیک‌نامی marks the object/goal of the striving ('for good repute'); به تن literally 'to/for the body' idiomatically means 'for oneself' ('for our person').
segments.s551-g228: کزین 'کز' + 'این' = 'from this/thereof' (introduces result/source). نام here means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame. یابیم is 1pl subjunctive 'may we find/attain/be known'. بر انجمن uses بر in a locative/relational sense 'in/front of the assembly' ('at gatherings').
segments.s551-g229: خنک at the start is a predicate adjective 'pleasant/refreshing/blessed' that introduces the following relative clause. آنک is a relative pronoun 'he who/that who'. جامی is 'a cup' (indefinite); بگیرد is 3rd-person singular subjunctive/optative of گرفتن 'takes/seizes', and به دست 'into the hand / in hand' specifies the manner/location.
segments.s551-g230: خورد here means 'befalls/comes upon' (not 'eat') — a common literary use meaning something happens to someone. یاد is the object 'remembrance/memory'; شاهان یزدان‌پرست is a genitive/attributive phrase 'of the God‑worshipping kings' modifying یاد.
segments.s551-g231: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. جام is 'cup'; نبیدش is 'his wine' (نبید = wine + possessive -ش). دمادم is an adverb 'constantly/continually'. شود is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of شدن 'become' and here yields 'when the cup of his wine becomes always (full/flowing)'.
segments.s551-g232: بخسپد is an archaic/poetic verbal form; in this context it functions as a 3rd‑person verb meaning roughly 'he falls asleep / lies down / becomes stupefied' (classical Persian خسبیدن). بدانگه 'thereupon/at that time' links the result clause; که is the conjunction 'that/when'; خرم is an adjective 'joyful/cheerful'; شود is 'becomes'. (Note: بخسپد is an old poetic form not given in modern lists.)
segments.s551-g201: A compound noun phrase listing two places. دگر = 'another' (adjective before the noun), بوم میسان = 'the land of Maysan' (بوم as 'land/soil' with a proper-name following), و connects the second noun رود فرات = 'the river Frat/Euphrates' (noun + proper name). The sequence is paratactic (simple listing) rather than using an izāfa link, typical in epic diction.
segments.s551-g202: A predicate nominal phrase using پر از + noun to mean 'full of ...'. پر (here adjective) + از + nouns in a series (چشمه و چارپای و نبات) lists what the places are full of; the conjunction و simply strings the items together.
segments.s551-g203: A noun phrase: دگر 'another' modifies شارستان 'town/city'. برکه اردشیر is an appositive/place-name 'the pond (or pool) of Ardashir' (noun + proper name). Word order puts the modifier 'دگر' before the whole place-name, a common poetic listing.
segments.s551-g204: Again uses پر از 'full of' to introduce a list: باغ (garden) and then another repeated پر 'full (of)' before گلشن for emphasis 'full of flower-gardens', with a final و joining آبگیر (reservoir/pond). Repetition of پر is a rhetorical/poetic device to intensify abundance.
segments.s551-g205: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. رام here is a place-name (not the lexical senses given), and اردشیرست is an archaic fused form meaning 'is Ardashir's / belongs to Ardashir' (copulative/possessive sense). شهری دگر is a resumptive apposition 'another city'. The word order compresses a full clause into a compact poetic phrase.
segments.s551-g206: A relative/adverbial clause 'from which (کزو) toward Pars I made passage'. کزو = 'from which', بر سوی = 'toward' (بر + سوی), پارس = 'Pars/Persia', کردم = 'I made/did' (past first person used here with گذر as the noun 'passage'), and گذر is the object/noun 'passage'. Word order is typical older Persian: relative + direction + verb + noun.
segments.s551-g207: A place-name phrase: دگر شارستان 'another town' followed by اورمزد اردشیر, a compound proper name (Ormazd‑Ardashir). The two nouns stand apposed to name the city; no linking particle is necessary in epic diction.
segments.s551-g208: Two coordinated descriptive clauses. هوا مشک بوی = 'the air (هوا) (was) musk-scented' where مشک بوی is a nominal phrase 'musk-scent' modifying هوا; the copula 'was' is understood. و joins the next phrase به جوی آب شیر = 'at/in the stream (جوی) [the] water (آب) [was] milk (شیر)', where به indicates location ('in/at') and آب شیر means 'water like milk' or 'milk-like water'. The verbs are omitted (ellipsis) and understood from context, a common poetic compression.
segments.s551-g209: روان مرا شادگردان به داد = an imperative request. روان (noun) is the thing to be gladdened; م is the clitic representing the 1st-person object (here part of مرا) and را is the definite/object marker attached after it; شادگردان is an imperative/causative verb meaning “make glad” (archaic/poetic form); به داد is a prepositional phrase ‘by/with justice’ where به functions as an instrumental/linker and داد is the abstract noun “justice.”
segments.s551-g210: که introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that / that”); پیروز is a predicate adjective “victorious” describing the addressee; بادی is an archaic/poetic optative/auxiliary form meaning “may you be” (not a literal ‘windy’ here); تو is the 2nd-person pronoun; بر تخت شاد is a prepositional phrase ‘on the joyous throne’ with بر = on/upon and شاد qualifying the throne.
segments.s551-g211: بسی رنجها بردم اندر جهان = statement of past experience. بسی is a quantifier meaning ‘much/many’ (classical usage); رنجها is the plural ‘sufferings/hardships’; بردم is 1st-person singular past of بردن ‘I bore/ endured’; اندر جهان is a locative phrase ‘in the world.’ Word order is flexible in classical Persian, but بردم is the main verb marking the past tense.
segments.s551-g212: چه ... و چه ... sets up a correlative (‘whether/in the open and whether/ in secret’ → here rendered ‘both ... and ...’). بر آشکار = ‘in the open/ openly’ with بر as a preposition and آشکار used adverbially; اندر نهان = ‘in secret/hidden’ with اندر = in and نهان used adverbially.
segments.s551-g213: کنون (now) opens the clause; دخمه را uses را as a definite/object marker attached to دخمه (the tomb); برنهادیم is the 1st-person plural past ‘we laid/placed on’ (برنهاد + ایم); رخت (clothes/garments) is the thing placed. Classical word order allows the locative/definite phrase دخمه را before the verb and the direct object رخت after the verb.
segments.s551-g214: This is a pair of imperatives addressed to ‘تو’ (you): بسپار is the imperative ‘entrust/commit’ taking تابوت (coffin) as its object; و links the commands; پرداز is an imperative ‘decorate/prepare’ (poetic/archaic form) taking تخت (throne) as its object. The subject ‘تو’ is explicit and governs both verbs.
segments.s551-g215: بگفت این = ‘he said this’ (بگفت = he said, این = this). و تاریک شد = ‘and became dark’ with شد = became; بخت اوی = ‘his fortune/destiny’ where بخت is ‘fortune/fate’ and اوی is the possessive clitic ‘his/her/its.’ The clause reports that his fate turned gloomy.
segments.s551-g216: دریغ is an exclamatory interjection ‘alas/pity’; آن points to the thing lamented ‘that’; the list سر و افسر و تخت (head and helmet and throne) names the objects of pity, and اوی at the end is the possessive ‘his,’ which here applies to the final noun and contextually to the group.
segments.s551-g9: چون شنودی is a temporal clause: چون + past/perfect 2nd-person form شنودی = 'when/after you have heard'. سخنهای من (literally 'my words') is the topic placed before the clause for emphasis, and بورز is a 2nd-person singular imperative 'do/put into practice' (imperative of ورزیدن). Word order is topical object → temporal clause → imperative.
segments.s551-g10: مگر here functions as 'unless'. بازدانی is a nominal/Verbal noun sense meaning 'restoration' or 'to restore' in this context ('unless you can restore/bring back'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. ناارز is a prefixed adjective 'unworthy/worthless' and ارز is 'value/worth' — the phrase literally 'unless you restore value from the unworthy' → naturalised as 'unless you can restore worth to what lacks it.'
segments.s551-g11: جهان راست کردم is a simple past statement: subject (جهان) + adjective (راست) + past verb کردم 'I made/put (it) right'. به شمشیر uses the preposition به in the instrumental sense 'by/with' and داد is a noun 'justice' — together 'by the sword of justice' (instrumental phrase placed after the verb).
segments.s551-g12: نگه داشتم is a compound verb meaning 'I kept/preserved' (نگه + past auxiliary داشتم = 'I kept/held'). ارج مرد نژاد is a genitival phrase 'the honour (ارج) of men (مرد) of lineage/stock (نژاد)' — object of the verb; word order is object after the verb, which is normal in older Persian when topicalised.
segments.s551-g13: چو here is the temporal 'when'. کار جهان is a genitive 'affairs of the world'. مر مرا is an emphatic double marking of the first person ('مر' = archaic dative 'to/for me' + 'مرا' = 'me' with را split orthographically here), and گشت راست is past 'became/turned right' (گشت = became; راست = right/well). Together: 'When the world's affairs became right for me.'
segments.s551-g14: فزون شد is a two-word predicate 'became more/increased' (فزون = increase/increasing, شد = became). زمین زندگانی is a noun phrase 'the earth's/world's livelihood/life', and بکاست is an archaic past-form meaning 'diminished/was lessened' used intransitively here (subject omitted because topicalised phrase precedes). The clause sets a contrast: prosperity rose yet the sustenance of the land waned.
segments.s551-g15: ازان پس که is a temporal sequence 'after that, when/seeing that'. بسیار بردیم رنج literally 'we bore much suffering' — بردیم is past tense 'we bore/undertook' (classical sense 'to carry/endure'), and بسیار modifies the verb as 'greatly/many (hardships)'. The clause = 'after that, when we endured many hardships'.
segments.s551-g16: به رنج is instrumental 'by/through hardship' (به + noun), اندرون 'within/inside' is the place where the action falls, گرد کردیم is a compound with کردیم 'we made/did' and گرد here functioning as a verbal element meaning 'to gather/collect' (گرد کردن = to gather), and گنج is the direct object 'treasure'. The clause means 'by hardships we collected/accumulated treasure within.'
segments.s551-g33: دو دیباست یک در دگر بافته is a compact nominal phrase: دو دیبا (two brocades/robes) with the copular/attributive ending -ست (here fused to دیباست) and the relative phrase یک در دگر بافته, literally “one woven in/into the other.” در is the locative/in-meaning and دگر modifies it (“the other”), while بافته is a past-participial adjective “woven,” describing how one brocade relates to the other.
segments.s551-g34: برآورده is a verbal/adjectival form meaning “brought forth / produced / provided” and here links the previous image to the following qualifier. پیش خرد places the action “before/in the presence of Wisdom” (پیش = in front of / before; خرد = wisdom). تافته is another past-participle/adjectival use from “weave/compose,” here reinforcing the idea of something being fashioned or woven (i.e., ‘fashioned’ or ‘woven’ in relation to خرد).
segments.s551-g35: This is a negative-clause with subject دین (religion) and predicate بی‌نیازست (is not without need / is not independent). از پادشا functions as a prepositional phrase 'from/of the king' indicating the source or relation (i.e., religion is not separate from the king). Word order places the negator نه at the start to negate the whole idea.
segments.s551-g36: This second negative clause parallels the first: نه introduces the negative, بی‌دین (without religion / impious) qualifies شاه (the king), بود is the copula ‘was/is,’ and آفرین is a noun meaning ‘praise’ or ‘acclamation.’ The structure means roughly ‘nor would a king be worthy of praise if he were without religion.’ Note the classical order (predicate before subject and object-marking را on شاه).
segments.s551-g37: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb ‘thus/so’; پاسبانان is the plural subject ‘guards’; یکدیگرند is a fused form meaning ‘are to one another’ / ‘are mutually so.’ The clause states a reciprocal relationship — these guards behave toward one another in the manner just described.
segments.s551-g38: تو گویی که is an idiomatic classical phrase meaning ‘one would say that’ / ‘it seems as if’; تو here is expletive/formulaic and گویی is the optative/quotative ‘one would say / it seems.’ که introduces the subordinate clause; در زیر یک چادرند literally ‘they are in/under one tent,’ a simile showing closeness/unity. Note the archaic idiom—تو گویی functions like an evidential/commentative particle.
segments.s551-g39: This is a paired negative construction: نه آن ... نه این ... meaning ‘neither that nor this.’ زین and زان are classical deictic/relational particles (here linking ‘from this’ / ‘from that’), and بود بی‌نیاز states the predicate ‘was not free from need / was not independent.’ Word order places the negatives up front and the predicate at the end, typical of older Persian.
segments.s551-g40: دو (two) modifies انباز, a rare/obscure word here (listed as uncertain); دیدیمشان is a compound verb meaning ‘we saw them’ (دیدیم + شان object clitic), and نیک‌ساز is an epithet ‘good-maker’/‘one who does good’ used as a substantive describing those people. The clause reports the poet’s observation of two such persons/figures.
segments.s551-g177: بپوشند is a third-person plural present-subjunctive/optative form (بـ- prefix + پوشند) used here as an optative/realizational 'may/they put on' or simply 'they put on'. The verb precedes the noun phrase (poetic word order). پیراهن is the direct object 'shirt/tunic' and بدتنی is an adjective after it meaning 'ill-favored/bad-bodied' modifying پیراهن.
segments.s551-g178: ببالند is likewise a 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/optative (بـ- + بالند) meaning 'they be proud / they boast'. با is the preposition 'with', followed by the noun phrase کیش آهرمنی = 'the creed/sect of Ahriman', where کیش 'creed' takes the genitive adjective آهرمنی 'Ahrimanic/demonic'. The clause links by coordination to the previous verb: 'they put on... and they boast with...'.
segments.s551-g179: گشاده شود is an optative/passive-like expression: گشاده 'open' + شود (3rd-sg) 'become / be (aux.)' → 'may it be opened' or 'let it be opened'. هرچ is a relative 'whatever', and ما بسته‌ایم is a present-perfect/compound ('we have closed' / 'we have bound') specifying what is to be opened. Word order places the optative verb phrase before the relative clause for poetic emphasis.
segments.s551-g180: بیالاید is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person-singular optative meaning 'may it come / may it appear'. آن دین 'that religion' is the object, and که introduces a relative clause 'که ما شسته‌ایم' — 'which we have washed/cleansed'. شسته‌ایم is a perfect (we have washed) used here figuratively as 'we have purified (the faith)'.
segments.s551-g181: تبه گردد is an optative/undesirable wish: تبه (here 'ruin/misfortune') + گردد (3rd-sg of گشت/گرد) 'become/turn' → 'may ... turn to ruin'. این پند و اندرز من is a coordinated noun phrase 'this counsel and admonition of mine' (پند and اندرز are synonymous nouns, followed by the possessor من). The verb governs the whole noun phrase as its subject/undergoer in the optative construction.
segments.s551-g182: به ویرانی آرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring to ruin' (به + ویرانی 'to destruction' + آرد 3rd-sg 'brings'). رخ این مرز من 'the face/appearance of this border/frontier of mine' is the direct object of آرد; رخ here means 'appearance/visage' and مرز 'border/frontier' takes the possessive من. Word order places the goal phrase (به ویرانی) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s551-g183: همی خواهم is a first-person verbal phrase where همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle and خواهم is 1st-sg present/future 'I will/wish'. از کردگار جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the Maker (کردگار) of the world (جهان)' — the one to whom the speaker addresses the petition.
segments.s551-g184: شناسنده is a participial noun 'knower' that apposes or modifies کردگار (i.e., 'the Maker, knower ...'). آشکار و نهان are paired adjectives 'manifest/clear and hidden' describing what the Maker knows. The construction is an appositive descriptive phrase: 'the Maker of the world, knower of the manifest and the hidden.'
segments.s551-g281: هوا روشن از بارور بخت اوی is a verbless poetic clause where هوا (subject) is linked to the predicate روشن. از here works as a causal preposition 'because of' (poetic use). بارور modifies بخت (an adjective before its noun in this poetic order), and اوی is the possessive/clitic referring back to the person (‘his’). The copula (is/was) is omitted as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s551-g282: زمین پایه نامور تخت اوی is another verbless clause: زمین is the topic/subject and پایه نامور تخت اوی is the predicate noun phrase 'the renowned foundation of his throne.' نامور modifies پایه; تخت + اوی is a possessed noun phrase ('his throne') where اوی is the possessive enclitic. The ezafe links are not written but understood in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s551-g283: به رزم اندرون ژنده پیل بلاست: به + noun + اندرون forms a locative phrase 'in/within the battle.' ژنده (an adjective 'ragged') directly modifies پیل ('elephant'). بلاست is a poetic third-person predication (noun + -ست) meaning 'is (a) calamity/scourge' — the copula and nominal predicate fused in the older poetic form.
segments.s551-g284: به بزم اندرون آسمان وفاست: parallels the first half with به بزم اندرون 'in the midst of the feast/assembly.' آسمان is the subject/figure, and وفاست is the predicate formed from وفا + -ست meaning 'is faithful' or 'is fidelity.' Again the copula is expressed in the older fused form -ست.
segments.s551-g285: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' در رزم is the locative 'in battle.' رخشان شود is a verbal phrase 'becomes bright/radiant' (شود = becomes, here present/future sense in narrative). رای اوی is the noun phrase 'his counsel/plan' (رای + possessive اوی). The clause order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: the predicate رخشان شود comes before the possessed noun رای اوی which it describes.
segments.s551-g286: همی موج خیزد ز دریای اوی: همی is an adverb 'ever/still/indeed' emphasizing the action. موج خیزد is an intransitive clause 'a wave rises' (خیزد = rises/appears). ز + دریای اوی is the source phrase 'from his sea' (ز poetic 'from'; دریای = 'sea' with possessive implied by اوی). The verb is third-person singular present/habitual.
segments.s551-g287: به نخچیر شیران شکار وی‌اند: به + noun phrase here signals the domain ('among/to the spoil of the lions' — نخچیرِ شیران). شکار is the predicate noun 'prey/quarry.' وی‌اند is an older third-person plural or impersonal present 'they are' (functioning here to assert existence/possession: 'are his'). The genitive relation ('نخچیرِ شیران') and the copula are condensed in poetic diction.
segments.s551-g288: دد و دام در زینهار وی‌اند: دد و دام is a coordinated subject 'enemy(s) and snares/traps.' در زینهار is a locative/relational phrase 'in/within (his) warning/admonition' (زینهار = warning/admonition). وی‌اند again functions as 'they are' (present/predicative), so the clause states that enemies and traps lie within his caution/warning — a compact poetic formulation.
segments.s551-g49: چه گفت is an interrogative phrase: چه (what) precedes the past verb گفت (said) to form 'what did ... say'. آن سخن‌گوی is a demonstrative (that) + agent noun (the speaker/eloquent one). با آفرین is an accompanying phrase ('with/with the acclaim “āfarīn”'), attributing praise to that speaker; the preposition با links the noun to the interjection/epithet آفرین.
segments.s551-g50: که introduces a reported-content clause ('that'). چون بنگری is a subordinate temporal/conditional clause: چون here = 'when/if' and بنگری is the present-subjunctive/imperative-form meaning 'you look' or 'if you look' (classical usage). مغز دادست دین is a poetically inverted predication: مغز (the core/essence) + دادست (an archaic copular/predicative form meaning 'is just/it is justice') + دین (religion), so the clause means 'the essence/core of religion is justice' with word order altered for meter and emphasis.
segments.s551-g51: سر تخت شاهی is a nominal phrase 'the top/head of the royal throne' (noun + noun + genitive/attributive), placed before the verb for poetic order. بپیچد is a present/classical form meaning 'turns/shall turn' (the b- prefix marking present/subjunctive in older style); سه کار is the grammatical subject ('three deeds'), so the clause literally reads 'three deeds turn the head of the royal throne.'
segments.s551-g52: نخستین is a nominalized adjective 'the first' pointing to the first of those deeds. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' marking source or cause. بیدادگر شهریار is a compound modifier 'a tyrant (bīdādgar) sovereign/king', so the phrase means 'the first (comes) from a tyrannical king.'
segments.s551-g53: دگر آنک begins a new enumerated clause: دگر = 'another' and آنک (archaic relative) = 'that which / he who'. بی‌سود را is the definite object ('the unprofitable/useless one') marked by را, and برکشد is a classical present/subjunctive/potential verb meaning 'he strikes/overthrows/dispatches' (here 'kills' or 'cuts down'); together: 'another [thing] is that he kills the unprofitable/useless one.'
segments.s551-g54: ز introduces the source/affected person: ز مرد هنرمند = 'from the skillful man' (i.e., the skilled man is the victim). سر درکشد is a poetical verb phrase where سر (head) is the direct object and درکشد (an older verb form) means 'he draws (out) / he removes' — together idiomatically 'he takes the head from (him)' = 'he beheads' or 'he deprives the skillful man of his head/life.' The subject is carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s551-g55: سه دیگر 'three others' introduces another group. که introduces a relative clause 'who'. با گنج خویشی کند uses با (with/by means of) + گنج (treasure/wealth) as the instrument, خویشی (kinship/affinity) as the thing made, and کند (does/makes) as the verb: 'who make relationships by means of treasure' (i.e., they buy or create kinship using wealth).
segments.s551-g56: به دینار کوشد is an instrumental phrase 'with dinar(s)/by money he strives' (به + noun indicating means), where کوشد is the present/subjunctive of کوشش کردن 'to try/strive'. که introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that', and بیشی کند = 'he may make/increase [more]' (بیشی = more/excess, کند = make/do). The whole clause says they use money and strive to get still more.

sentences.s551-s137: At the head of the book I composed praise of him—his greatness, his custom, and his counsel.
sentences.s551-s138: From him I saw in the world a good name; may his end be auspicious in the world.
sentences.s551-s139: Because of seeing him the crown has become bright; because of evil things his fortune has become a cuirass for him.
sentences.s551-s140: May pious people take pride in him — that very one who became king upon the earth.
sentences.s551-s121: From the smallest chaff up to the lofty Throne, God is witness throughout all existence.
sentences.s551-s122: Call none but Him—the Maker of the world, knower of the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s551-s123: From Him, upon the soul of Muhammad, blessings; He increased them for each of his companions.

sentences.s551-s29: Turn to generosity, religion, and wisdom, and let no lie at all pass by you.
sentences.s551-s30: A king’s countenance becomes dark from falsehood; its loftiness never gains radiance.
sentences.s551-s31: Beware, so that you are not the guardian of the treasure, for people, because of dinars, rush into hardship.

sentences.s551-s49: Do not put off today's work until tomorrow; do not seat a corrupter on the throne.
sentences.s551-s50: Do not seek truth from the hearts of common folk, for by searching you will bring yourself loss.
sentences.s551-s51: If ill news comes to you from them, do not listen to slanderers, and let sorrow not consume you.
sentences.s551-s52: Be neither a flatterer of kings nor a worshipper of God; if you seize the foot, the head will come into your hand.
sentences.s551-s57: When your secret is revealed in the city, the hearts of the wise grow merciless.
sentences.s551-s58: You stir things up, and they will call you light‑headed; if a wise man seats you before him...

sentences.s551-s136: May his name remain eternal; may every preeminence be his destiny.

sentences.s551-s94: Blessings from God and from us upon that person whose tar is wisdom and whose weft is justice.
sentences.s551-s95: May no defeat befall this covenant of mine; may no one try to turn my honey into colocynth.

sentences.s551-s45: One should not pursue two pastimes at once — wine and feasting, hunting and going out.
sentences.s551-s46: For the body becomes wearied by constant seeking; the elders kept these words in mind.
